headline: Notre Dame's ground game overwhelms Arkansas in wire-to-wire rout
body: Notre Dame controlled this game from the opening drive, establishing a physical advantage on both lines that Arkansas never recovered from. The Irish defense limited Arkansas to 310 total yards while forcing the Razorbacks into a perpetually one-dimensional attack. Arkansas quarterback Jackson completed just 13 of 30 passes for 249 yards with no touchdowns, a reflection of constant pressure and the need to chase points early.

Hollywood Smothers became the game's most disruptive force, punching in two rushing touchdowns while carrying the ball 14 times. That workload wasn't accident—Notre Dame's coaching staff recognized Arkansas couldn't stack the box without surrendering vertical space, and the Irish O-line validated that read by establishing a 145-yard rushing day. Smothers' second touchdown, which put Notre Dame up 28-10 midway through the fourth quarter, effectively ended any narrative about a comeback.

The most telling stat sits in the passing game disparity. Notre Dame quarterback Carr managed 24 completions on 39 attempts for 357 yards and two scores, benefiting from receivers who had separation and a ground game that dictated tempo. Arkansas receiver Clay actually posted solid yardage with 91 yards on four catches, but lacked the support cast and offensive rhythm needed to sustain drives. Notre Dame's defense, anchored by Lucas's seven tackles, never allowed Arkansas to establish anything resembling momentum.
turningPoint: Notre Dame's second touchdown drive in the second quarter extended the lead to 14-3 and signaled Arkansas would need to abandon its conservative approach, a transition that only widened the gap.

unsungHero.name: Lucas
unsungHero.why: Seven tackles anchored the Irish defense without the splash of a sack or turnover, the kind of steady two-level football that allowed Smothers to operate freely in space and set the physical tone that defined the game.
